naming_inspiration.name_element: Pierpont (given to son Jared Pierpont Brockett)
naming_inspiration.description: Son Jared Pierpont Brockett (b. Mar 18, 1810) was given the Pierpont name as a middle name to honor Timothy's mother's family (the Pierpont line). The maternal-surname-as-middle-name was a deliberate naming convention preserving the Pierpont ancestry forward across this branch.

narrative_blocks.header: Built the first tannery in Saratoga, New York
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Brockett built the first tannery in Saratoga New York — when the place was a frontier settlement of log huts. The tannery was the founding industrial establishment of what became one of New York's noted nineteenth-century resort and industrial towns. He had reached Saratoga by sailing up the Hudson River to Albany, then crossing to Schenectady (then a small town), then taking the bridle path north to Saratoga.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children including farmer and merchant sons
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy and Olive Root had eight children: Lucy (b.1794), Polly (b.1796 d.1853 never married), Abel (1799-1803 died young), Laura (b.1803 d.1803 died young), Hannah (b.1804 married Justus Eli Allen of Oxford NY), Thomas (b.1806 farmer in Black River County NY), Jared Pierpont Brockett (b. March 18, 1810), and Ira Brockett (b.1812 married Mary Mairs, successful merchant). Two of the eight died as infants.

narrative_blocks.header: Named son Jared Pierpont for mother's family
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy gave his fifth surviving son the middle name Pierpont — Jared Pierpont Brockett — honoring his own mother's Pierpont family ancestry. The maternal-surname-as-middle-name was a deliberate genealogical naming convention common to early-nineteenth-century New England families seeking to preserve memory of mother's surname forward across generations of children who would otherwise carry only the father's line.

naming_inspiration.name_element: Pierpont (given to son Jared Pierpont Brockett)

The tannery was the founding industrial establishment of what became one of New York's noted nineteenth-century resort and industrial towns. cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

bio.bio_blurb: North Haven farmer with twelve children

military_service.notes: FindAGrave: Lieut Joseph Pierpont; North Haven farmer.

narrative_blocks.header: Double sibling marriage joined Pierponts and Russells
narrative_blocks.body: He married Hannah Russell around 1725 while his sister Mary married Hannah's brother William the same year. Two siblings on each side, married into a single family in one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His marriage joined two Yale-founding families
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. James Pierpont was a Yale founding trustee in 1701. His wife's father Rev. Noadiah Russell was another.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children at his North Haven farm
narrative_blocks.body: He farmed land about ten miles northeast of New Haven through the 1730s and 1740s. Several of his twelve children died in infancy, including the first James in 1727.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — Yale founding trustee 1701
